transcript:
GAP (HOLD-APART) TRANSCRIPT - instance examples/field-guide, run r4/r4.1, proposal p2-r4-compose-restructure
Seat: hold-apart (the Gap, xor). Role: derive held-out witnesses Fiat-Shamir from the proposal artifact. No merit judgement.

=== STEP 1 - NUMBERED FACT CENSUS OF THE ORIGINAL GUIDE ===
Source: artifact/GUIDE.md, body split into sentences on . ! ? boundaries in reading order; every sentence stating a checkable fact indexed F1 upward. 32 facts (F1..F32), matching frontier.json best.evidence census.

F1: This guide explains how to prepare an ordinary household to manage safely and comfortably through a power outage lasting up to seventy-two hours.
F2: It is written for a temperate climate and assumes no backup generator.
F3: Work through the sections in order; the earlier sections cover the preparations that matter most.
F4: Store four litres of water per person per day, which means twelve litres per person for the full seventy-two hours.
F5: Half of that allowance is for drinking, and the other half covers cooking and basic hygiene.
F6: Store the water in food-grade containers, keep the containers out of direct sunlight, and replace stored water every six months.
F7: If a storm is forecast and you have advance warning, also fill the bathtub; bathtub water is for flushing toilets and washing, never for drinking.
F8: Keep a dedicated shelf of non-perishable food sufficient for three days.
F9: Good choices are canned beans, canned fish, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and shelf-stable milk.
F10: Choose foods that require no cooking, and make sure you own a manual can opener, because an electric one will be useless.
F11: Eat the perishable food from the refrigerator first, then the food from the freezer, and only then open the emergency shelf.
F12: An unopened refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours.
F13: A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about forty-eight hours, but a half-full freezer holds it for only about twenty-four hours, so it is worth keeping the freezer consolidated and full, even if some of the space is taken up by containers of frozen water.
F14: Keep both doors closed as much as possible and tape a note to each door as a reminder.
F15: Any perishable food that has spent more than two hours above four degrees Celsius should be discarded; when in doubt, throw it out.
F16: Use battery-powered LED lanterns or headlamps as your primary light source, and avoid candles entirely, since candles are a leading cause of house fires during outages.
F17: Store at least one light per person, plus two spare sets of batteries for each light.
F18: Keep one headlamp beside each bed and one lantern in the kitchen, and place them where they can be found in the dark.
F19: Never run a camping stove, barbecue, or generator indoors, in a garage, or near a window, because each of these produces carbon monoxide, which is odourless and can be fatal.
F20: Install at least one battery-powered carbon monoxide alarm on each floor where people sleep.
F21: In cold weather, choose one small room on a south-facing side of the home, close its door, and gather everyone there; layered clothing and blankets are safer than any improvised heater.
F22: If the indoor temperature falls below ten degrees Celsius and you cannot maintain warmth, relocate to a warming centre or a neighbour's home.
F23: Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts, and know the frequency of your local emergency station before the outage begins.
F24: Charge every phone and power bank whenever a storm is forecast; a full modern power bank of twenty thousand milliamp-hours will recharge a typical phone roughly four times.
F25: Put your phone in low-power mode at the start of the outage, and agree in advance on one out-of-area contact whom every member of the household will text, because text messages get through congested networks more reliably than voice calls.
F26: Keep a first-aid kit and a seven-day supply of every prescription medication, and rotate the medications so they stay in date.
F27: Refrigerated medication such as insulin generally remains usable at room temperature for up to twenty-eight days, but confirm the rule for your specific medication with a pharmacist before an emergency happens.
F28: Write down the phone numbers of your pharmacy, your doctor, and the poison-control line on paper, because a dead phone is not a phone book.
F29: Wait five minutes before switching major appliances back on, so the grid can stabilise.
F30: Check the freezer: if food still contains ice crystals, it can be safely refrozen.
F31: Restock everything used during the outage within one week, starting with water and batteries.
F32: Finally, write a one-page note about what was missing or awkward, and fix at least one of those gaps before the next outage arrives.

=== STEP 2 - CANONICAL SERIALIZATION AND SEED ===
The proposal artifact (verbatim in the prompt) was JSON-parsed, then re-serialized with recursively sorted object keys and no whitespace (canonical JSON). Top-level keys sorted: compressedText, expectedMetric, hardConstraintNote, killedLeverCitations, lens, leverId, rationale, title. killedLeverCitations is a 1-element array preserved in order. Result written verbatim to proposal_canon.json (5730 bytes) in this run dir; those exact bytes are the ONLY input hashed.
Hash command (run in the run dir, Git Bash):
    sha256sum proposal_canon.json
Output:
    b733dbd6dcabd64c2c47d53fb528c4be71c6b987c59041ece336de6f98ae10c5 *proposal_canon.json
seedHex = b733dbd6dcabd64c2c47d53fb528c4be71c6b987c59041ece336de6f98ae10c5
VERIFY: any third party who canonicalizes the same proposal artifact (recursive sorted keys, no whitespace) and runs sha256sum reproduces this digest. If sha256sum proposal_canon.json != seedHex, the round is void.

=== STEP 3 - DRAW RULE (8 distinct facts, no replacement) ===
Digest read left to right as unsigned bytes b0,b1,...  remaining = ordered list [F1..F32]. For draw k: idx = b_k mod (count of remaining); remove remaining[idx] (0-based) and record it. 8 draws.
Digest bytes (hex): b7 33 db d6 dc ab d6 4c ...  => b0=183 b1=51 b2=219 b3=214 b4=220 b5=171 b6=214 b7=76
k=0: count=32, idx=183%32=23 -> remaining[23]=F24; remove F24
k=1: count=31, idx=51%31=20 -> remaining[20]=F21; remove F21
k=2: count=30, idx=219%30=9 -> remaining[9]=F10; remove F10
k=3: count=29, idx=214%29=11 -> remaining[11]=F13; remove F13
k=4: count=28, idx=220%28=24 -> remaining[24]=F29; remove F29
k=5: count=27, idx=171%27=9 -> remaining[9]=F11; remove F11
k=6: count=26, idx=214%26=6 -> remaining[6]=F7; remove F7
k=7: count=25, idx=76%25=1 -> remaining[1]=F2; remove F2
DRAWN (order): F24, F21, F10, F13, F29, F11, F7, F2 - 8 distinct.

=== STEP 4 - COMPREHENSION QUESTIONS (expected answers quoted from the ORIGINAL sentence) ===
[
  {
    "factIndex": "F24",
    "question": "When should you charge every phone and power bank, and how many times will a full modern 20,000 mAh (twenty thousand milliamp-hour) power bank recharge a typical phone?",
    "expectedAnswer": "Charge every phone and power bank whenever a storm is forecast; a full modern power bank of twenty thousand milliamp-hours will recharge a typical phone roughly four times."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F21",
    "question": "In cold weather, which single room should you gather everyone in, and what does the guide say is safer than any improvised heater?",
    "expectedAnswer": "In cold weather, choose one small room on a south-facing side of the home, close its door, and gather everyone there; layered clothing and blankets are safer than any improvised heater."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F10",
    "question": "What must you make sure you own for your food supply, and why will an electric version be no use?",
    "expectedAnswer": "Choose foods that require no cooking, and make sure you own a manual can opener, because an electric one will be useless."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F13",
    "question": "How many hours does a full freezer hold a safe temperature, and how many hours does a half-full freezer hold it?",
    "expectedAnswer": "A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about forty-eight hours, but a half-full freezer holds it for only about twenty-four hours, so it is worth keeping the freezer consolidated and full, even if some of the space is taken up by containers of frozen water."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F29",
    "question": "When power returns, how long should you wait before switching major appliances back on, and why?",
    "expectedAnswer": "Wait five minutes before switching major appliances back on, so the grid can stabilise."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F11",
    "question": "In what order should you eat your stored food during the outage?",
    "expectedAnswer": "Eat the perishable food from the refrigerator first, then the food from the freezer, and only then open the emergency shelf."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F7",
    "question": "When should you also fill the bathtub, and what may that bathtub water be used for and not used for?",
    "expectedAnswer": "If a storm is forecast and you have advance warning, also fill the bathtub; bathtub water is for flushing toilets and washing, never for drinking."
  },
  {
    "factIndex": "F2",
    "question": "What climate and what equipment assumption is this guide written for?",
    "expectedAnswer": "It is written for a temperate climate and assumes no backup generator."
  }
]

Witnesses derived solely from the proposal hash; the proposer neither saw nor influenced them (T2/GR-4). proposal_canon.json left in place for re-derivation.
